body:
in Film |   	March 19th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Jim Jarmuschãthe direcôÙtor of Stranger Than ParôÙadise, Down by Law, and Dead ManãrecentôÙly stepped into The CriôÙteôÙriôÙon ColôÙlecôÙtion closôÙet to share the films that shaped his aesôÙthetôÙic senôÙsiôÙbilôÙiôÙty. In the next three minôÙutes, JarôÙmusch pays tribôÙute to a box set of Pier PaoôÙlo Pasoliniãs 1960s films, along with Andrei Tarkovskyãs MirôÙror, placôÙing it even above StalkôÙer and Andrei Rublev. Along the way, he name checks a numôÙber of othôÙer clasôÙsics. And then, with a bag full of DVDs in hand, he conôÙcludes: ãI love CriôÙteôÙriôÙon. I always say the CriôÙteôÙriôÙon ChanôÙnel is my drug of choice.ã For anyôÙone interôÙestôÙed, you can start watchôÙing films on the CriôÙteôÙriôÙon ChanôÙnelãs streamôÙing serôÙvice by signôÙing up for a free triôÙal here.

title: How Quentin Tarantinoãs One-Night ãDetest Festã Changed His Life & Set Him on the Path to Pulp Fiction
description:
in Film, Life |   	March 20th, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

Just days ago, a game came out whose unlikeôÙly premise has already drawn a good deal of att
body:
in Film, Life |   	March 20th, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

Just days ago, a game came out whose unlikeôÙly premise has already drawn a good deal of attenôÙtion. ãManôÙage your very own video store in the earôÙly 90s!ã exclaims the descripôÙtion of Retro Rewind. ãRent, sell, decôÙoôÙrate and expand your busiôÙness from the ground up and relive the goldôÙen ages of video rentals!ã Those of us old enough to have relied on such estabôÙlishôÙments for our earôÙly cinôÙeôÙmatôÙic eduôÙcaôÙtion can all too easôÙiôÙly rememôÙber how frusôÙtratôÙing they could be, what with their physôÙiôÙcalôÙly limôÙitôÙed selecôÙtions, selôÙdom-rewound tapes, and puniôÙtive late fees. Even so, younger genôÙerôÙaôÙtions arenãt wrong to imagôÙine that some were speôÙcial places where it felt like a cinephileãs dreams could come true. Just ask Quentin TaranôÙtiôÙno.
The clip above comes from Joe Roganãs interôÙview with TaranôÙtiôÙno and Roger Avary, who worked togethôÙer at ManôÙhatôÙtan Beachãs Video Archivesô before they co-wrote Pulp FicôÙtion. ãWorkôÙing at that store, I just got caught up in the litôÙtle life there,ã TaranôÙtiôÙno says. Yet he also rememôÙbers himôÙself thinkôÙing, ãWell, this isnãt my dream. This isnãt what I wantôÙed to do workôÙing at a video store for years. I wantôÙed to actuôÙalôÙly make movies. Itãs not my dream, what Iãm doing ã but itãs dream-adjaôÙcent!ãô It turned out that getôÙting paid to watch movies all day long (to say nothôÙing of becomôÙing localôÙly famous for sheer cinephilôÙia) withôÙout putting in any seriôÙous manôÙuôÙal labor ãput my ambiôÙtions to sleep a litôÙtle bit.ã
TaranôÙtiôÙno explains that his awakôÙenôÙing from this retail reverôÙie began with witôÙnessôÙing the sudôÙden embitôÙterôÙment of felôÙlow clerks who passed the age of thirôÙty doing the same ãcoolã jobs they always had. This set him on the path to underôÙgoôÙing a series of dark nights of the soul he called ãQuentin detest fests,ã durôÙing which he would make a no-excusôÙes accountôÙing of all the misôÙtakes he was activeôÙly or pasôÙsiveôÙly makôÙing. ãI would spend all night layôÙing out everyôÙthing Iãm doing thatãs wrong, and then I would spend the last two hours figôÙurôÙing out how I could change it. And as opposed to just doing it and then going to get some sleep, and then you forôÙget about it and fall back into your rouôÙtine, I decidôÙed to change my life.ã
AttachôÙment to his job was a big part of the probôÙlem. ãIãve got to just move to HolôÙlyôÙwood, Iãve got to get involved there, Iãve got to meet othôÙer peoôÙple that are in the busiôÙness,ã he realôÙized. ãI shouldôÙnãt be makôÙing monôÙey until Iãm makôÙing monôÙey doing what I want to do.ã Not long after reloôÙcatôÙing from the South Bay to KoreôÙatown ã still well south of HolôÙlyôÙwood, but close enough ã he startôÙed makôÙing conôÙnecôÙtions in the low-budôÙget horôÙror world. ãWell, if these guys can do it, I can do it,ã he came to believe, and withôÙin a year and a half he was makôÙing a livôÙing as a screenôÙwriter.ô The video rental indusôÙtry has long since colôÙlapsed, but Quentin TaranôÙtiôÙno is still going strong as a filmôÙmakôÙer. If he takes a break from workôÙing on what may be his last picôÙtureô to play Retro Rewind, weãd sureôÙly all be interôÙestôÙed in hearôÙing what memôÙoôÙries it brings back. Maybe he and Avary can disôÙcuss it on theirô Video Archives PodôÙcast.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:

Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Alan Lomaxãs Massive Music Archive Is Online: Features 20,000 Historic Blues & Folk Recordings
description:
in Music |   	March 19th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
A huge treaôÙsure trove of songs and interôÙviews recordôÙed by the legôÙendary folkôÙlorist Alan L
body:
in Music |   	March 19th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
A huge treaôÙsure trove of songs and interôÙviews recordôÙed by the legôÙendary folkôÙlorist Alan Lomax from the 1940s into the 1990s has been digôÙiôÙtized and made availôÙable online for free lisôÙtenôÙing. The AssoôÙciôÙaôÙtion for CulôÙturôÙal EquiôÙty, a nonôÙprofôÙit orgaôÙniôÙzaôÙtion foundôÙed by Lomax in the 1980s, has postôÙed some 20,000 recordôÙings.
ãFor the first time,ã CulôÙturôÙal EquiôÙty ExecôÙuôÙtive DirecôÙtor Don FlemôÙing told NPRãs Joel Rose, ãeveryôÙthing that weãve digôÙiôÙtized of Alanãs field recordôÙing trips are online, on our Web site. Itãs every take, all the way through. False takes, interôÙviews, music.ã
Itãs an amazôÙing resource. For a quick taste, here are a few examôÙples from one of the best-known areas of Lomaxãs research, his recordôÙings of traôÙdiôÙtionôÙal African AmerôÙiôÙcan culôÙture:

ãJohn HenôÙryã sungô by prisôÙonôÙers at the MisôÙsisôÙsipôÙpi State PenôÙiôÙtenôÙtiary, ParchôÙman Farm, in 1947.
ãCome Up Horsey,ã a chilôÙdrenãs lulôÙlaôÙby sung in 1948 by Vera Hall, whose mothôÙer was a slave.
ãIn a ShanôÙty in Old ShanôÙty Townã perôÙformed by Big Bill Broonzy, 1952.
ãStoôÙry of a slave who asked the devôÙil to take his masôÙter,ã told by Bessie Jones in 1961.

But thatãs just scratchôÙing the surôÙface of whatãs inside the enorôÙmous archive. Lomaxãs work extendôÙed far beyond the Deep South, into othôÙer areas and culôÙtures of AmerôÙiôÙca, the Caribbean, Europe and Asia. ãHe believed that all culôÙtures should be looked at on an even playôÙing field,ã his daughôÙter Anna Lomax Wood told NPR. ãNot that theyãre all alike. But they should be givôÙen the same digôÙniôÙty, or they had the same digôÙniôÙty and worth as any othôÙer.ã
You can lisôÙten to Roseãs pieceô about the archive on the NPR webôÙsite, as well as a 1990 interôÙview with Lomax by TerôÙry Gross of Fresh Air, which includes samôÙple recordôÙings from Woody Guthrie, JelôÙly Roll MorôÙton, Lead BelôÙly and MisôÙsisôÙsipôÙpi Fred McDowôÙell. To dive into the Lomax audio archive, you can search the vast colôÙlecôÙtion by artist, date, genre, counôÙtry and othôÙer catôÙeôÙgories.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post origôÙiôÙnalôÙly appeared on our site in March 2012.

If you would like to supôÙport the misôÙsion of Open CulôÙture, conôÙsidôÙer makôÙingô a donaôÙtion to our site. You can conôÙtribute through PayôÙPal, PatreôÙon, and VenôÙmo (@openculture). Thanks!

title: An Introduction to the Strait of Hormuz and Its Role in the Longstanding US-Iran Conflict
description: Above, you can watch a primer on the Strait of HorôÙmuz, the narôÙrow pasôÙsage between Iran and Oman through which roughôÙly 20% of the worldãs
body:
Above, you can watch a primer on the Strait of HorôÙmuz, the narôÙrow pasôÙsage between Iran and Oman through which roughôÙly 20% of the worldãs oil supôÙply flows. ProôÙduced by Vox, the video explains why this chokeôÙpoint has long played a cenôÙtral role in tenôÙsions between the UnitôÙed States and Iran. Since the 1980s, Iran has threatôÙened to disôÙrupt trafôÙfic through the Strait, all as a way to exert presôÙsure on the globôÙal econôÙoôÙmy. Now, facôÙing an attack from the UnitôÙed States and Israel, itãs makôÙing good on its threats, slowôÙing trafôÙfic to a trickôÙle. With oil prices surgôÙing, the Trump adminôÙisôÙtraôÙtion has yet to demonôÙstrate that it has a coherôÙent plan for counôÙterôÙing a stratôÙeôÙgy that Iran announced decades ago. Stay tuned for moreãÎ

title: Marc Fornes' New Sculptural Pavilion Reimagines the Architectural Folly
description: A bold new structure has appeared in Cary Park in Cary, North Carolina: the latest sculptural pavilion by Marc Fornes / THEVERYMANY. The work is t
body:
A bold new structure has appeared in Cary Park in Cary, North Carolina: the latest sculptural pavilion by Marc Fornes / THEVERYMANY. The work is titled ãLãIle Folie,ã which nods to the architectural tradition of the folly, a landscape feature that was all the rage with wealthy estate owners in the 18th and 19th centuries.
Often nostalgic and resembling ruined miniature castles or bucolic village buildings, follies were generally non-functional and conceived as pure decoration. Fornes, however, reimagines this practice with an eye toward the future rather than the past. The pavilion ãgives this tradition a contemporary meaning:ô memorable, playful, and slightly surreal,ã says a statement.

Fornes is known for creating high-tech structures made from thousands of individual facets, blurring the distinction between architecture and sculpture. Situated along a boardwalk and perched over a pond, the gleaming white pavilion invites visitors to pause and appreciate their natural surroundings from a contemporary landmark.
ãConstructed from ultra-thin folded aluminum panels, each piece is digitally fabricated and precisely riveted into place,ã says a statement. ãThere is no hidden frame; the skin is the structure. Thousands of perforations filter sunlight into delicate patterns, turning the canopy into an ever-changing atmosphere of shadow and shimmer.ã
See more on THEVERYMANYãs Instagram and Vimeo.

schema.headline: Marc Fornes' New Sculptural Pavilion Reimagines the Architectural Folly

title: Breezy Swathes of Fabric Dance Amid Landscapes in Thomas Jackson's Photos
description: Multi-colored lengths of fabric billow in the breeze in the work of Thomas Jackson, challenging the relationship between nature, human interventio
body:
Multi-colored lengths of fabric billow in the breeze in the work of Thomas Jackson, challenging the relationship between nature, human intervention, and consumerism. ãRooted in the tension between nature and artificiality, the installations pose questions about how we interact with the environment and how we might find equilibrium with it,ã the artist writes in a statement.
ãAll of my photographs strain credulity by design,ã Jackson says. ãAt first blush, they can appear to be digital fabrications, but in truth, they are entirely in-camera, printed with minimal post-production.ã The undulating swathes of fabric in his ethereal photographs initially appear to float and drape on their own, almost superimposed onto various landscapes, but upon closer inspection, the images reveal taut strings holding the carefully layered sheets.

Currently, Jackson is focusing on a forthcoming book project and taking advantage of the time between major exhibitions to make new work. See more on Instagram.

schema.headline: Breezy Swathes of Fabric Dance Amid Landscapes in Thomas Jackson's Photos

title: Ben Zankãs Portraits Teeter Between Surrealism and the Mundane
description:
Tensely contorted or standing pin straight, Ben Zankãs signature faceless subjects evoke ineffable yet familiar emotions. 
The New York City-based
body:
Tensely contorted or standing pin straight, Ben Zankãs signature faceless subjects evoke ineffable yet familiar emotions. 
The New York City-based photographer has a knack for turning ordinary settings and unaccompanied figures into strangely perplexing sights. Mismatched socks, bold garments, and awkward poses go a long way in evoking a visceral response through his lens, tapping into a sort of uncanny realism.

Zankãs work traveled to Festival Cargo Les Photographiquesãa.k.a. The Cargo Festivalãin Saint-Nazaire, France last summer. Since its debut in 2022, the annual event typically features several outdoor exhibition areas, highlighting contemporary photographers.
The artistãs plein air installation for the festival included large reproductions of images affixed to leaning wooden pallets and covering brick walls. The minimalist, Earth-toned portraits complemented their surroundings, scattered across sparse grounds.
Zank is currently working on a series that is focused on capturing strangers in their own homes, rather than the constructed settings he typically employs. Find more from the artist on Instagram.

schema.headline: Ben Zankãs Portraits Teeter Between Surrealism and the Mundane
